nativeRange.habitat: Ponds and forest creeks rich in plant debris; slow or still blackwater over soft substrate with heavy leaf litter and submerged wood

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate at least 3–5 cm deep for burrowing
dried leaf litter (oak or beech leaves) for camouflage and refuge
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
South American biotope planting (Amazonian swords, java fern, anubias)

diet.notes: Nocturnal sit-and-wait micro-predator feeding on small invertebrates and organic matter along the substrate. Accepts live and frozen bloodworms, earthworms, tubifex, and brine shrimp; will take quality sinking pellets and wafers over time. Feed after lights out. Does not compete well at feeding time with faster tankmates — target feeding is often required.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (e.g., neon, ember, cardinal)
avoidWith: boisterous fast-moving species that outcompete at feeding
species requiring bright lighting or high water movement

imageCandidates.notes: Kacper Aleksander; 5184x3456. Sharp full-body front view of a live specimen showing the broad flattened banjo-shaped head, tapering body, barbels and tubercled scaleless skin.
Kacper Aleksander; 5184x3456. Live fish partly buried in sand, illustrating its leaf-litter camouflage and granular tubercled skin; sharp on the fish.

summary: The Banjo Catfish is a flat, leaf-shaped catfish from the Amazon basin that spends its days motionless and nearly invisible among leaf litter on the substrate. Strictly nocturnal and moderately challenging to keep, it requires careful feeding management and is sensitive to copper-based medications.
careNotes:
The Banjo Catfish earns its name from a distinctly flat, banjo-shaped body — a broad, flattened head tapering to a slender tail — covered in tubercle-studded, scaleless skin that mimics leaf litter on the substrate. A purely nocturnal sit-and-wait predator, it will remain motionless and all but invisible during daylight hours, emerging only after lights out to forage. A fine sand substrate at least 5 cm deep is important for this species, which will partly bury itself; dried leaf litter (oak or beech leaves) and bogwood are strongly recommended to provide daytime cover and replicate its natural habitat. Dim or diffuse lighting is preferred, as intense lighting tends to cause prolonged hiding and feeding refusal.

Because the skin is scaleless and heavily textured, the Banjo Catfish is sensitive to copper-based medications and should not be treated with standard copper or organophosphate treatments. Copper-free alternatives are advised, and any medication should be dosed cautiously. Soft, acidic to neutral water (pH 5.8–7.8, hardness 2–20 gH) replicates Amazonian blackwater conditions; adding dried leaf litter or filtering through peat is beneficial. The older scientific name Dysichthys coracoideus is still encountered in older aquarium literature; it was synonymised with Bunocephalus following a revision of the family Aspredinidae, and Bunocephalus coracoideus (Cope, 1874) is the currently accepted name.

Target-feed after lights out with live or frozen bloodworms, earthworms, or brine shrimp. This species will not compete with daytime feeders and can go underfed in a busy community tank if feeding is not actively managed. Groups of three or more are recommended, as conspecifics are well tolerated and the fish tends to behave more naturally in the company of others. Care is rated intermediate because of the nocturnal feeding demands, copper sensitivity, and the need for a soft, low-light biotope setup.
breedingNotes: Rarely achieved in captivity. Requires a group of at least six mature specimens and a species or biotope tank with fine sandy substrate. Spawning occurs at night on the substrate; the female deposits 4,000–5,000 eggs onto sandy substrate, which hatch in approximately three days. Fry accept microworm or Artemia nauplii immediately after absorbing the yolk sac. No parental care is provided. Sexual dimorphism is subtle — ripe females appear more rounded in the abdomen.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Bunocephalus coracoideus
primary.notes: Primary source. Minimum aquarium 80 cm per FishBase guidance.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Bunocephalus coracoideus
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Aspredinidae, Siluriformes, described by Cope 1874), max length (11 cm SL), native countries (Bolivia, Brazil, Peru), habitat (ponds and creeks with plant debris), diet ('bottom-feeder, consuming debris and smaller fish'), spawning (up to 4,000 eggs into sandy substrate), aquarium popularity, IUCN Least Concern. No explicit mention of Dysichthys synonymy on Wikipedia page; Friel 1995 synonymy documented per primary task brief.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding is rarely achieved in captivity. A group of at least six mature specimens is needed, along with a fine sand substrate and a quiet biotope tank. The female deposits 4,000 to 5,000 eggs on the sandy substrate at night; eggs hatch in approximately three days. Fry accept microworm and Artemia nauplii.
